claim.hypotheses_location: Section 4: The current research (page ~380): Predictions that mortality salience decreases utilitarian responses to conflict dilemmas and has no comparable effect on control scenarios.
claim.statement_location: Abstract and title (page ~379); Discussion (pages ~382–383) state that mortality salience reduces utilitarian responses and that its effect is comparable to very high cognitive load.

data.source: Laboratory vignette judgments/questionnaire responses from university participants
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 85 participants. Experiment 2: 115 participants recruited; 5 outliers removed, final N = 110.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant judgments of moral dilemmas (percentage/frequency of utilitarian responses)
data.notes: Participants recruited on campus at the University of Toulouse. Mortality salience manipulated via death vs. extreme pain thought prompts. Scenarios adapted from high-conflict moral dilemmas but framed as non-lethal harm to avoid inducing mortality salience in controls. Order of scenarios counterbalanced. Pretest with 58 independent participants used to select two scenarios (Crying Baby, Captive Soldier) with conflict acceptability ≥ 50% and near-100% acceptability in control versions. In Experiment 2, cognitive load manipulated with Dot Memory Task (3x3 easy, 3x3 difficult at 850 ms, 4x4 very difficult at 2000 ms). Five multivariate outliers identified via Mahalanobis distance and removed.

method.description: Two experiments tested whether mortality salience (MS) reduces utilitarian moral judgments and whether its impact parallels cognitive load. Participants were randomly assigned to think about death (MS) or extreme pain (control), then judged non-lethal harm moral dilemmas in conflict and control versions. Experiment 2 additionally imposed varying spatial working-memory loads before each dilemma via the Dot Memory Task. Analyses compared frequencies of utilitarian responses across conditions using ANOVAs and t-tests.
steps: Recruit participants on campus; obtain consent.
Randomly assign participants to Mortality Salience (Death) or Control (Pain) condition.
Mortality salience manipulation: Answer two open-ended prompts about own death (Death) or extreme pain (Pain) per Greenberg et al. (1990).
Present four non-lethal harm moral scenarios (two conflict, two control; Crying Baby, Captive Soldier) with counterbalanced order; record moral acceptability judgments (utilitarian vs deontic).
Experiment 2 only: Before each scenario, administer a Dot Memory Task matrix (low/high/very high load) to be recalled after judgment; record dot recall performance.
Compute percentage/frequency of utilitarian responses per participant and condition.
Experiment 1 analysis: ANOVA on utilitarian response frequency with problem type (conflict vs control) and condition (Death vs Pain); follow-up t-tests.

Experiment 2 analysis: ANOVA with problem type, condition, and cognitive load; for conflict problems, ANOVA with condition and load; post hoc comparisons; remove multivariate outliers via Mahalanobis distance.
method.models: ANOVA with interaction terms; independent-samples t-tests; post hoc pairwise t-tests; outlier detection via Mahalanobis distance.
method.outcome_variable: Frequency/percentage of utilitarian responses to moral dilemmas (primarily in conflict problems).
method.independent_variables: Mortality salience condition (Death vs Pain); Problem type (conflict vs control); Cognitive load level (low, high, very high; Experiment 2).
method.control_variables: Scenario version (conflict vs control); order counterbalancing; non-lethal harm framing to avoid confounding MS in controls; removal of multivariate outliers (Experiment 2).

results.summary: Across two experiments, mortality salience reduced the frequency of utilitarian responses on conflict dilemmas but not on control dilemmas. Experiment 1 showed a significant interaction between problem type and condition, with fewer utilitarian responses under mortality salience for conflict problems. Experiment 2 replicated the mortality salience effect and further showed that very high cognitive load similarly reduced utilitarian responses by approximately 20 percentage points, whereas low and high load did not differ. No interaction between mortality salience and cognitive load was detected for conflict problems, suggesting additive or comparable effects.

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
